coredata.dc:title: An artificial intelligence handheld sensor for direct reading of nickel ion and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid in food samples using ratiometric fluorescence cellulose paper microfluidic chip
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Liru
Zhang, Bianxiang
Zhou, Wei
Hao, Jiaxin
Shi, Hu
Wang, Shuo
Shuang, Shaomin
Shi, Lihong
coredata.dc:description: User-friendly in-field sensing protocol is crucial for the effective tracing of intended analytes under less-developed countries or resources-limited environments. Nevertheless, existing sensing strategies require professional technicians and expensive laboratory-based instrumentations, which are not capable for point-of-care on-site analyses. To address this issue, artificial intelligence handheld sensor has been designed for direct reading of Ni2+ and EDTA in food samples. The sensing platform incorporates smartphone with machine learning-driven application, 3D-printed handheld device, and cellulose paper microfluidic chip stained with ratiometric red-green-emission carbon dots (CDs). Intriguingly, Ni2+ introduction makes green fluorescent (FL) of CDs glow but red FL fade because of the coordination of Ni2+ with CDs verified by density functional theory (DFT), concurrently manifesting continuous FL colour transition from red to green. Subsequent addition of EDTA renders FL of CDs-Ni2+ recover owing to the capture of Ni2+ from CDs by EDTA based on strong chelation effect of EDTA on Ni2+ confirmed via DFT, accompanying with a noticeable colour returning from green to red. Inspired by above FL phenomena, CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chips are first fabricated to facilitate point-of-care testing of Ni2+ and EDTA. Designed fully-automatic handheld sensor is utilized to directly output Ni2+ and EDTA concentration in water, milk, spinach, bread, and shampoo based on wide linear ranges of 0–48 μM and 0–96 μM, and low limits of detection of 0.274 μM and 0.624 μM, respectively. The proposed protocol allows for speedy straightforward on-site determination of target analytes, which will trigger the development of automated and intelligent sensors in near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artificial intelligence
Handheld sensor
Machine learning
Cellulose paper microfluidic chip
Ni2+ and EDTA
Ratiometric carbon dots
Intelligent analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FL detection of Ni2+ and EDTA
Construction of artificial intelligence handheld sensor
Preparation of cellulose paper microfluidic chip
User-friendly in-field quantification of different concentrations of Ni2+ and EDTA through artificial intelligence handheld sensor
DFT calculation of FL sensing mechanism
Artificial intelligence handheld sensor for user-friendly in-field quantitation of Ni2+ and EDTA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of cellulose paper microfluidic chip. (B) Photos of the cellulose paper microfluidic chip under daylight. (C) Preparation of CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip. (D) Schematic illustration of artificial intelligence handheld sensor.

(A–C) TEM images of CDs under diverse magnifications. (Inset in Fig. 1A is size distribution of CDs. Inset in Fig. 1C is HRTEM image of CDs). (D) AFM topographic image of CDs. (Inset is the distribution of height profile following a white line). (E) AFM 3D image of CDs. (F) Height distribution of CDs.
(A) CDs' XPS total spectrum. (B–E) C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p spectrum of CDs. (F) CDs' FTIR spectrum.

(A) CDs' FL spectra and colour variation in response to various concentrations of Ni2+ (0–48 μM) at 385 nm excitation. (B) Linearity correlationship between FL intensity ratios (I632/I526) and Ni2+ contents at 0–48 μM. (C) Determined CIE coordinate from FL spectra of CDs with various Ni2+ contents. (D) FL spectra and colour variation of CDs-Ni2+ in response to diverse EDTA contents (0–96 μM) under 385 nm excitation. (E) Linearity correlationship between FL intensity ratios (I632/I526) and EDTA concentrations at 0–96 μM. (F) Determined CIE coordinate from FL spectra of CDs-Ni2+ with diverse EDTA contents.

Energy diagrams of HOMO and LUMO of CDs (A) and EDTA (B) before and after addition Ni2+. (C) Energy diagrams of LUMO and HOMO of CDs-Ni2+ before and after addition EDTA. (D) The △Ead diagram of CDs-Ni2+ and EDTA-Ni2+.
Schematic illustration of YOLO v3 ML algorithm.
The use flowchart of “Artificial Intelligent Assay”. (Green arrow is the fitting steps, red arrow is prediction steps).
Artificial intelligence handheld sensor for user-friendly in-field quantification of Ni2+ and EDTA.
Artificial intelligence handheld sensor for user-friendly in-field quantitation of Ni2+ in various water, spinach, and milk.
Artificial intelligence handheld sensor for user-friendly in-field quantitation of EDTA in various water, shampoo, and bread.

head.ce:title: An artificial intelligence handheld sensor for direct reading of nickel ion and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid in food samples using ratiometric fluorescence cellulose paper microfluidic chip

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Automation and Software Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Automation and Software Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Automation and Software Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: User-friendly in-field sensing protocol is crucial for the effective tracing of intended analytes under less-developed countries or resources-limited environments. Nevertheless, existing sensing strategies require professional technicians and expensive laboratory-based instrumentations, which are not capable for point-of-care on-site analyses. To address this issue, artificial intelligence handheld sensor has been designed for direct reading of Ni2+ and EDTA in food samples. The sensing platform incorporates smartphone with machine learning-driven application, 3D-printed handheld device, and cellulose paper microfluidic chip stained with ratiometric red-green-emission carbon dots (CDs). Intriguingly, Ni2+ introduction makes green fluorescent (FL) of CDs glow but red FL fade because of the coordination of Ni2+ with CDs verified by density functional theory (DFT), concurrently manifesting continuous FL colour transition from red to green. Subsequent addition of EDTA renders FL of CDs-Ni2+ recover owing to the capture of Ni2+ from CDs by EDTA based on strong chelation effect of EDTA on Ni2+ confirmed via DFT, accompanying with a noticeable colour returning from green to red. Inspired by above FL phenomena, CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chips are first fabricated to facilitate point-of-care testing of Ni2+ and EDTA. Designed fully-automatic handheld sensor is utilized to directly output Ni2+ and EDTA concentration in water, milk, spinach, bread, and shampoo based on wide linear ranges of 0–48 μM and 0–96 μM, and low limits of detection of 0.274 μM and 0.624 μM, respectively. The proposed protocol allows for speedy straightforward on-site determination of target analytes, which will trigger the development of automated and intelligent sensors in near future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: By virtue of powerful computational capability, high resolution camera, speedy data-transmission, and high coverage, smartphone has been employed for hand-held quantitation of target analytes [1,2]. Currently, smartphone-integrated hand-held sensing device consisting of smartphone, 3D-printed optical attachment, and ratiometric fluorescent (FL) paper probe has proved its efficacy for on-site visual quantitation [3]. Wherein, ratiometric FL paper probe endows visual determination ability [4], smartphone makes the quantitative determination realize through tracking hue-saturation-value (HSV) and red-green-blue (RGB) values of ratiometric FL paper probe [5], and 3D-printing provides satisfactory portable detection device with UV light and functional slots [6]. Our team identified Hg2+, Mn(VII), and GSH through the smartphone-integrated hand-held sensing device via single ratiometric FL carbon dots (CDs) [7,8]. Exploiting the device, Jiang's group detected GSH by mixing CDs with Au nanoclusters and determined thiram based on the mixtion of blue-emission CDs and red-emission CdTe quantum dots (QDs)@silica nanoparticles [9,10]. Zhang et al. exploited dual-emitting QDs hybrid for on-site identifying F− through the device [11]. Wang et al. combined NH2-rich EuMOF with fluorescein 5-isothiocyanate to in-situ recognize biogenic amines by the device [12]. Guo et al. mixed o-phenylenediamine and Ag nanoparticles/UiO-66-NH2 to construct ratiometric FL probe for onsite quantitative determination of glucose and cholesterol employing the device [13]. Nonetheless, the smartphone-integrated hand-held sensing device does not read out results directly but requires cumbersome manual data processing, which fails to achieve automated determination.

Due to robust autonomous learning ability from large databases on own convolutional neural networks, machine learning (ML), a computer algorithm, can process data automatically without human intervention [14,15]. As a representative model in ML, YOLO v3 exhibits strong anti-interference capability, satisfactory recognition accuracy, and rapid recognition speed for target analytes by combining the multi-scale fusion prediction method with the residual network structure [16]. The introduction of YOLO v3 ML algorithm to the smartphone-integrated hand-held sensing device may accomplish automated analysis, which is considered as a promising sensing protocol [17]. Presently, utilizing the AI-assisted smartphone-integrated hand-held sensing device, we achieved point-of-care analysis of F− via Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced red-emission enhanced CDs [18]. Adopting the device, Lu's team determinated Cu2+, Hg2+, thiram, sulfide, and tetracycline antibiotics by constructing multi-colour ratiometric FL sensor [19–22]. With the assistance of the device, Liu et al. developed polychromatic ratiometric FL sensors for in-field identifying GSH and azodicarbonamide by mixing CDs and 2,3-diaminophenazine [23]. Nevertheless, the research of automated sensor is in the initial stage and thus will still stand for a vigorous research area.

Nickel ion (Ni2+) is an essential microelement in biological system that plays a pivotal role in enzyme activity, intra-uterine development, and iron adsorption of the human body [24]. Nevertheless, superfluous ingestion of Ni2+ could bring about multiple diseases, including asthma [25], pneumonitis [26], dermatitis [27], cardiovascular disease [28], central nervous system disorder [29], and nasal and lung cancer [30]. Consequently, it is extremely imperative to exploit a point-of-care effective protocol for Ni2+ identification. Additionally, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) is universally acknowledged as not only powerful chelator and clarificant that is widely applied for treatment of acute heavy metal poisoning [31], but also convenient commercial antioxidant and antiseptic that is frequently utilized in food manufacture as well as fast food [32]. However, excessive intake of EDTA may result in multiple free radicals, trigger obesity, increase load of kidney and other organs [33], induce allergic dermatitis [34], and bring about hypocalcaemia, hypomagnesaemia, and hypozincaemia in blood [35]. Thus, it is particularly significant to exploit credible methods for user-friendly in-field efficacious identification of EDTA.

Typically, Microfluidic paper chip is fabricated through constructing miniaturized fluidic channels on the cellulose paper substrate, which can effectively confine and guide 0.1 to 100 μL liquids flow [36]. Currently, cellulose paper microfluidic chip has been proven to be an ideal platform for simultaneous determination of multiple analytes owing to inherent characteristics of microfluidic devices and paper. Martinez et al. exploited cellulose paper microfluidic chip by patterning photoresist onto chromatography paper for simultaneous measurement of glucose and protein, wherein glucose detection is according to the enzymatic oxidation of iodide to iodine and protein determination is based on the colour variation of tetrabromophenol blue [37]. Tong et al. developed a laser-printed cellulose paper microfluidic chip on basis of polychromatic FL CDs for on-site determination of multiple antibiotics simultaneously [38]. Zhang et al. designed a wax-printed cellulose paper microfluidic chip based on a graphene oxide functionalized single-stranded DNA sensor to simultaneously detect different types of chemical contaminants in food [36]. Nevertheless, incorporating cellulose paper microfluidic chip with the highly-intelligent fully-integrated sensor for simultaneously quantification of various chemical analytes have not been reported.

Herein, an artificial intelligence handheld sensor with direct reading fashion based on smartphone with machine learning (ML)-driven self-programming application, 3D-printed optical attachment, and cellulose paper microfluidic chip loaded with ratiometric red-green-emission CDs has been ingeniously designed for user-friendly in-field quantification of Ni2+ and EDTA (Scheme 1 ). CDs were prepared using alizarin carmine and p-phenylenediamine via hydrothermal treatment, manifesting intriguing dual-emission behavior peaked at 526 and 632 nm under 385 nm excitation. With increasing Ni2+ concentration, FL intensity of CDs at 526 nm moderately raises while that at 632 nm significantly declines, along with continuous FL colour variation of red to green. With subsequent introduction of EDTA, FL of CDs can be ratiometric restored, simultaneously exhibiting a continuous FL colour return from green to red. Based on the sensing phenomena, CDs can be extended as ratiometric FL cellulose paper microfluidic chip to in-field recognize Ni2+ and EDTA, owing to the fantastic linear correlationship between R/G values and the content of Ni2+ and EDTA. Furthermore, employing the automated handheld sensor, user-friendly in-field quantification of Ni2+ and EDTA in real samples can be fulfilled on the basis of wide linear ranges as well as low limits of detection (LOD). The developed sensing methodologies will provide a brand-new perspective for food safety screening and environmental monitoring.

P-phenylenediamine (0.50 g) and alizarin carmine (0.05 g) were added into 30 mL ultrapure water, and subsequently such a cloudy solution was transferred to 50 mL autoclave which reacted at 200 °C for 4 h. Later, the crude product was purified through centrifuging at 3000 rpm for 20 min, filtration with 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzing with 500–1000 Da dialysis membrane for 12 h. Finally, acquired CDs were collected and preserved in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL detection of Ni2+ and EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: For Ni2+ determination, 50 μL CDs solution (0.57 g L−1) was homogeneously distributed into 2000 μL phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.4) at 0.014 g L−1 final content, and then different standard levels of Ni2+ solution were individually introduced. FL measurements were performed under an excitation wavelength of 385 nm.

Similarly, for EDTA identifying, various standard levels of EDTA solution were separately diluted into CDs-Ni2+ system (0.014 g L−1 CDs, 48 μM Ni2+). FL measurements were implemented under an excitation wavelength of 385 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of artificial intelligence handheld sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Artificial intelligence handheld sensor mainly includes three components: 3D-printed optical attachment, ratiometric FL cellulose paper microfluidic chip, and smartphone with built-in self-designed APP involving YOLO v3 ML algorithm function. The specifications and usage descriptions of 3D-printed attachment have been previously reported by our team [8]. The self-designed APP “Artificial Intelligent Assay” consists of two functions of fitting and predicting. The data set information extraction code of the APP is presented in below link: https://github.com/zzwna/Artificial-Intelligence-Assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cellulose paper microfluidic chip
ce:section.ce:para: The pattern of paper microfluidic chip was first plotted utilizing AutoCAD 2020 software (Fig. 1A). Subsequently, Whatman No. 1 cellulose filter paper was attached to A4 paper, and further printed the designed pattern on it using the laser toner printer. Then, the printed filter paper was heated for 60 min at 200 °C employing the high-temperature oven to construct the hydrophobic barriers on paper, namely, nine test zones, nine hydrophilic micro-channels, nine buffer zones, and the hydrophilic tip. Afterward, the printed filter paper was naturally cooled to ambient temperature and subsequent removed from the oven (Fig. S1). Finally, prepared cellulose paper was cut and collected for further exploration (Fig. 1B).
CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip was fabricated via dipping hydrophilic tip into 0.014 g L−1 CDs solution (≥50 μL) for 10 min (Fig. 1C) and then air-dried at ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: User-friendly in-field quantification of different concentrations of Ni2+ and EDTA through artificial intelligence handheld sensor
ce:section.ce:para: First, different concentrations of Ni2+ and EDTA were respectively dropped on the test zones of CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip and air-dried at ambient temperature. Then, the test zones of CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip was cut and placed in sample cell of ML-integrated smartphone-based handheld sensor. Finally, FL signals of CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip were documented through artificial intelligence handheld sensor (Fig. 1D).

TEM images (Fig. 2A–C) clearly and intuitively exhibit that CDs have quasi-spherical appearance and exceptional dispersity. Inset in Fig. 2A depicts that the size distribution of CDs ranges from 1.60 to 4.0 nm and even particle size is calculated as 2.80 ± 0.50 nm. The interlayer lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm is observed through HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 2C), corresponding to the (102) face of graphitic carbon [39]. AFM images (Fig. 2D–E) demonstrate that CDs have outstanding dispersity and the average statistical height is measured to be 3.09 nm (Fig. 2F), corroborating a total consistency with TEM findings.

CDs' XPS total spectrum displayed in Fig. 3A presents four noticeable bands located at 528.9 eV, 395.3 eV, 281.3 eV and 165.1 eV pertaining to O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p, respectively. CDs' C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) is differentiated into three bands at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 289.1 eV, belonging to existence of CC, C−O and O=C−O, respectively. CDs' O1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) is separated into three bands at 531.4 eV, 532.2 eV, and 533.4 eV that could be classified as CO, C−OH, and C−O−C, respectively. N1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3D) is distinguished as two peaks, that is, C−N (399.6 eV) and N−H (400.8 eV). S2p spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3E) presents three peaks of S2p3/2 of C−S, S2p1/2 of C−S, and CS that are located at 167.7 eV, 168.9 eV and 170.4 eV, respectively [21]. Meanwhile, FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3F) illustrates that there contain O−H (3397.8 cm−1), N−H (3166.2 cm−1 and 1520.1 cm−1), C−H (2870.0 cm−1), S−H (2365.2 cm−1), COO− (1793.2 cm−1), CC (1671.5 cm−1), C−N (1354.9 cm−1), C−O−C (1144.9 cm−1), C−S (1069.2 cm−1), C−O (950.8 cm−1), and =C−H (855.0 cm−1) on the CDs' surface. Great agreement is gained with XPS analysis. The aforementioned results indicate that CDs' surface contains carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, and sulfur-containing groups.

UV–vis spectrum of CDs (Fig. S2A) presents three chief absorption peaks centered at 204.7 nm, 240.7 nm and 303.6 nm. The peak at 204.7 nm arises from π−π* transition of CC of CDs core state [40]. The peaks at 303.6 and 240.7 nm are assigned to n−π* transition of C=O/C−S and n−σ* transition of N−H/O−H on CDs surface state, respectively [41]. Additionally, it is found that the solution of CDs is pink under sunlight and red under 385 nm irradiation (inset of Fig. S2A). FL spectra depict that CDs engender amusing dual-emission behavior at 526 nm and 632 nm (Fig. S2A). Such well-resolved emission peaks endow CDs with ratiometric determination potential, which can effectively reduce the interference and improve the detection precision. Meanwhile, FL quantum yield of CDs is calculated up to 9.2 % using rhodamine B as a reference and FL lifetime of CDs is determined as 6.07 ns (Fig. S2B).

To develop practical application, CDs' optical stability was tracked. Various concentrations of NaCl (0–4 M), continuous irradiation for 150 min and storage for 30 days exhibit negligible influence on FL intensity of CDs displayed in Fig. S3A–C. Above phenomena illustrate superior optical stability, which endues CDs with tremendous prospect in practical application.

CDs' selectivity was evaluated by investigating the influences of various ions and amino acids under 385 nm excitation. As demonstrated in Fig. S4A–C, only Ni2+ renders FL intensity ratio (I632/I526) significantly decrease while other analytes exhibit trifling effect, implying that CDs possess superior specificity toward Ni2+ and could be utilized to selectively discriminate Ni2+. Furthermore, other interfering substances with high content (up to 48 μM) show negligible effect on the identification of Ni2+ (Fig. S4A–C).

Determination capability of CDs for Ni2+ in the scope of 0–48 μM was inspected under 385 nm excitation at pH 7.4. With increasing Ni2+ concentration, FL intensity of CDs at 632 nm drops gradually while that at 526 nm ratiometric rises (Fig. 4A), during which FL colour variation is presented progressively from red to green (inset in Fig. 4A). A fantastic linear correlationship with R2 = 0.9882 is presented between FL intensity ratios (I632/I526) and Ni2+ content in the scope of 0–48 μM (Fig. 4B). Determined LOD is 3.6 nM according to signal/noise ratio of 3 and considerably less than previously reported sensors (Table S1), suggesting superior sensitivity of CDs for Ni2+. FL colour of CDs with various Ni2+ concentrations is precisely defined by Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4C), manifesting a total agreement with FL colour of CDs-Ni2+ system. Such results demonstrate quite feasibility of CDs for visual Ni2+ identification.

Selectivity of CDs-Ni2+ was further investigated by exploring the influences of representative metal ions, anions and amino acids. Except for EDTA, other analytes demonstrate minimal influences on FL intensity ratio of CDs-Ni2+ (Fig. S5A–C), implying that CDs-Ni2+ has fantastic selectivity toward EDTA and could be applied to selectively recognize EDTA. Additionally, FL intensity ratio of CDs-Ni2+ remains stable even after addition of other interfering substances up to 96 μM, which confirms excellent anti-interference capability of CDs-Ni2+ (Fig. S5A–C).

Determination ability of CDs-Ni2+ for EDTA in the scope of 0–96 μM was studied under 385 nm excitation at pH 7.4. Accidentally, FL intensity of CDs-Ni2+ at 526 nm incrementally drops while that at 632 nm progressively rises as EDTA concentrations increases (Fig. 4D), simultaneously exhibiting a successive FL colour transformation of green to red (inset in Fig. 4D). Such an outcome demonstrates admirable feasibility of CDs-Ni2+ for visual recognition of EDTA. FL intensity ratio expresses a good linear correlationship with EDTA concentrations in the scope of 0–96 μM (Fig. 4E). A LOD of 7.8 nM is calculated according to the signal/noise ratio of 3, suggesting that superior sensitivity of proposed sensor to previously reported EDTA probes (Table S2). Additionally, CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4F) was also utilized to record FL colour variation trend of CDs-Ni2+ in response to EDTA in concentration scope of 0–96 μM, which exhibits good consistency with FL colour of CDs-Ni2++EDTA solution. Such outcome manifests that visual identification of EDTA is of quite feasibility.

Ni2+ possesses unoccupied orbital and is prone to accepting electrons. CDs' surface possesses carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, and sulfur-containing groups, in which O of carboxyl groups contains lone pair electrons. Consequently, as Ni2+ is added into CDs solution, lone pair electrons of O can enter into the dsp2 hybrid orbits of Ni2+ to form new complexes based on the theory of coordination chemistry. To confirm the proposed sensing mechanism, zeta potential and FTIR spectra of CDs were investigated in the existence of Ni2+. As demonstrated in Fig. S6, when Ni2+ is introduced zeta potential of CDs system increases remarkably from −16.30 mV to −8.51 mV, which may result from the consumption of carboxyl anions on CDs' surface and thereby confirm the successful coordination between Ni2+ and CDs. Furthermore, comparing with FTIR spectrum of CDs in Fig. S7, two new absorption bands at 1781.6 cm−1 and 1399.8 cm−1 emerge in that of CDs-Ni2+, further proving the combination of CDs with Ni2+. To verify the binding group between the CDs and Ni2+, XPS spectrum of CDs-Ni2+ was measured. In contrast to XPS spectrum of CDs in Fig. 3A, the Ni2p peak located at 855.3 eV appears in XPS spectrum of CDs-Ni2+ (Fig. S8A). Ni2p spectrum of CDs-Ni2+ is separated into two peaks at 851.9 eV and 853.5 eV (Fig. S8B), pertaining to Ni2p3/2 and Ni2p1/2, implying the presence of Ni2+. C1s, N1s, and S2p spectra of CDs-Ni2+ have no obvious changes compared with that of CDs (Fig. S8C–E). However, the emergence of a new peak at 531.8 eV in the O1s spectrum of CDs-Ni2+ is attributed to the presence of Ni−O bond, indicating that the coordination reaction mainly occurs between Ni2+ and -COOH on CDs surface to form a NiO bond (Fig. S8F). Based on above results, the binding group of coordination reaction between CDs and Ni2+ is Ni−O bond. Simultaneously, as Ni2+ is added the even diameter of CDs increases obviously from 2.80 to 11.20 nm (Fig. S9A–B) and the even height of CDs increases significantly from 3.09 nm to 11.02 nm (Fig. S10A–B), illustrating that the coordination between Ni2+ and CDs induces CDs' agglomeration. On one hand, the formation of CDs-Ni2+ complex weakens red FL resulting from static quenching, which is implied by almost unchanged fluorescence lifetime and varying UV–vis absorption (Fig. S11A–B). On the other hand, Ni2+-triggered agglomeration restricts free rotation of functional groups on CDs' surface, prevents nonradiative transitions, promotes radiative transition, and thus enhances green FL.

The electron-donating ability of EDTA is much stronger than CDs. Upon adding EDTA to CDs-Ni2+ system, EDTA could competitively capture Ni2+ from CDs' surface and further chelate with Ni2+. Zeta potential and FTIR were implemented to corroborate the proposed sensing mechanism. On adding EDTA to CDs-Ni2+, zeta potential of CDs-Ni2+ decrease remarkably from −8.51 mV to −14.7 mV (Fig. S6) and two new absorption bands at 1781.6 cm−1 and 1399.8 cm−1 disappear in comparison with FTIR spectrum of CDs-Ni2+, revealing that the departure of Ni2+ from CDs' surface and the formation of new EDTA-Ni2+ complex (Fig. S7). Furthermore, upon introducing EDTA into CDs-Ni2+, the even diameter of CDs-Ni2+ declines from 11.20 nm to 3.52 nm (Fig. S9C–D) and average height of CDs-Ni2+ drops from 11.02 nm to 7.61 nm (Fig. S10C–D), which further validates the release of aggregation. Therefore, the capture of Ni2+ from CDs surface by EDTA prevents energy transfer between CDs and Ni2+ recovering red FL, and the release of aggregation prevents radiative transition and enhances nonradiative transition, declining green FL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculation of FL sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To better grasp the ratiometric FL sensing mechanism of CDs toward Ni2+ or EDTA, density functional theory (DFT) calculation was implemented adopting the RB3LYP/6-31G(D) theory through Materials Studio [42]. Schematic diagrams of CDs and EDTA are displayed in Fig. S12A–B, respectively. Molecular frontier orbital theory can be utilized to analyze the difficulty of reaction and the practical reaction active center of molecules. The lower energy of LUMO the stronger capability of accepting electrons. Inversely, the higher energy of HOMO the stronger ability of donating electrons [43]. Additionally, the energy gap of HOMO and LUMO can be utilized to illustrate the compound's stability, in which the lower energy gap indicates the more stable compound [44]. According to the outcome of DFT calculations, energy gap of CDs-Ni2+ (△2 = 1.6477 eV) is far less than that of CDs (△1 = 3.1876 eV) (Fig. 5A), and energy gap of EDTA-Ni2+ (△4 = 4.0979 eV) is far less than that of EDTA (△3 = 5.9357 eV) (Fig. 5B), confirming that both CDs and EDTA may combine with Ni2+. Inspiringly, the difference between △3 and △4 (△b = −1.8378 eV) is significantly lower than that between △1 and △2 (△a = −1.5399 eV) (Fig. 5A–B), showing that EDTA is easier to combine with Ni2+ to form more stable EDTA-Ni2+ chelate than CDs. Furthermore, as EDTA is introduced into CDs-Ni2+ system the lower energy gap of CDs-(Ni2+-EDTA) system (△5 = 0.00657 eV) is obtained than that of CDs-Ni2+ system (△2 = 1.6477 eV), further verifying that EDTA can take away Ni2+ from CDs' surface (Fig. 5C). Additionally, the adsorption energy (△Eab) also is calculated between Ni2+ and CDs as well as EDTA. As displayed in Fig. 5D, △Eab of CDs-Ni2+ is −15.4797 eV while △Eab of EDTA-Ni2+ is −18.6872 eV, corroborating that EDTA is prone to coordination with Ni2+ because of the better chemical adsorption between Ni2+ and EDTA.

To further improve efficiency and practicability of highly-intelligent fully-integrated sensor, a self-designed APP “Artificial Intelligent Assay” was developed. The APP adopts YOLO v3 ML algorithm to intelligently recognize the cellulose paper microfluidic chip images, and further combines with the images processing algorithm to segment and extract HSV and RGB signal values. To increase the accuracy of the APP recognition of the object paper probes, a total of 800 FL images are collected including 60 % training set and 40 % test set, and data enhancement is simultaneously utilized to expand cellulose paper microfluidic chip category with a total of 22 categories according to voc2007 training set format. Firstly, the effective region of the object cellulose paper microfluidic chip in images is labeled utilizing the label what you see (LWYS) technology, and the size of object cellulose paper microfluidic chip is adjusted to 416 × 416 that is input to the Darknet-53 feature extraction network. Next, the trained YOLO v3 ML algorithm is employed for prediction, and the picture annotation tool is utilized to label basic truth boundary frames. Finally, the RGB and HSV value of the segmented region is further extracted. Schematic illustration of YOLO v3 ML algorithm extraction process for FL cellulose paper microfluidic chip images is presented in Fig. 6 . YOLO detection layers and Darknet-53 backbone network structure are the main components of the algorithm model, in which the detection layers have three prediction feature layers and the Darknet-53 network structure possesses 53 convolution layers. The first predicted feature layer is 13 × 13, on which global target information of the images can be predicted. Furthermore, the target information of medium-scale can be predicted in the 26 × 26 feature layer and the small target information can be predicted in the 52 × 52 feature layer. Besides, YOLO v3 ML algorithm has an improving accuracy for target detection by introducing the residual network structure, which can avoid gradient dispersion when the network is deepened.

To confirm the practicability of the trained YOLO v3 ML algorithm model, the various parameters including precision, recall, and F1-score were employed to evaluate the performances of the model in identification and location the target paper microfluidic chip. The calculation formula of precision, recall, and F1-score are as follows:
(1)
precision =
TP
TP + FP
(2)
recall =
TP
TP + FN
(3)
F 1 =
2 × precision × recall
precision + recall
wherein, TP (true positive) represents the actual paper microfluidic chip tested via the YOLO v3 model, FP (false positive) represents the target mistaken for the paper microfluidic chip through the model, and FN (false negative) represents the undetected paper microfluidic chip through the model. The evaluation results of the YOLO v3 model are presented in Table S3, the precision is calculated to be 94.8 %, the recall rate reaches 100.0 % and F1 does 97.3 %. Above results exhibit superior accuracy of YOLO v3 ML algorithm, which can be utilized to precisely identify and locate the object paper microfluidic chip.

The use instructions of “Artificial Intelligent Assay” are displayed in Fig. 7 , including fitting (following the direction of the green arrow) and predicting (following the direction of the red arrow). During fitting, two picture acquisition methods of “image upload” and “real-time shooting” could be selected, of which “image upload” be adopted in our work. The imported picture can be automatically recorded via the YOLO v3 algorithm. HSV and RGB values of the documented picture can be extracted and presented in the interface of APP. The relationships between various signal parameters and the concentrations of Ni2+ (Fig. S13) as well as EDTA (Fig. S14) are intelligently fitted by APP. Wherein, a fantastic linear correlationship with R2 = 0.9721 is plotted between R/G and Ni2+ concentration in the range of 0–48 μM (Fig. S15A), and LOD is determined as 0.274 μM, which is lower than the maximum concentration of Ni2+ (1.20 μM) in drinking water permitted by the World Health Organization (WHO) [45]. Simultaneously, R/G and EDTA concentration in the scope of 0–96 μM present a good linear correlationship with R2 = 0.9835 (Fig. S15B) and the LOD of 0.624 μM is gained, which is less than the maximum level of EDTA (2.05 μM) in drinking water allowed through the WHO [46]. Above results exhibit a total agreement with fluorescence spectrophotometry, illustrating that “Artificial Intelligent Assay” APP can be generalized to intelligently and speedily quantify Ni2+ and EDTA through the signal parameter of R/G.
On uploading cellulose paper microfluidic chip picture and selecting most suitable linearity correlation, the unknown content of Ni2+ and EDTA in actual samples can be directly read out (Fig. S16).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Artificial intelligence handheld sensor for user-friendly in-field quantitation of Ni2+ and EDTA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip for Ni2+ was determined via dropping different cations solution. Only Ni2+ renders FL colour of CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip obviously shift of red to green (Fig. S17) under UV light, which reveals excellent specificity of CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip for Ni2+ over competitive cations. Selectivity of CDs-Ni2+-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip for EDTA was further appraised by introducing various solutions of anions into CDs-Ni2+-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip. EDTA renders FL colour of CDs-Ni2+-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip basically restore from green to red while other anions have negligible influence (Fig. S18), illustrating that CDs-Ni2+-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip could be utilized to specific recognize EDTA.

Furthermore, to validate the accuracy of analytical results, dimethylglyoxime spectrophotometric method and eriochrome black T method were employed to measure Ni2+ and EDTA, respectively. As shown in Tables S4 and S5, the relative errors between the proposed method and standard method are <5 %. These results verify the proposed method can be utilized to quantify Ni2+ and EDTA with high accuracy.

Ratiometric red-green-emission CDs were fabricated by facile one-pot hydrothermal method. Continuous introduction of Ni2+ and EDTA to CDs solution gives rise to intrinsic ratiometric FL transformation (I632/I526). Concomitantly, a successive FL colour transition from red to green to red sets the stage for user-friendly in-field quantification of Ni2+ and EDTA by artificial intelligence handheld sensor. Furthermore, the CDs-based cellulose paper microfluidic chip is first employed for ultrafast automated quantification of Ni2+ and EDTA in diverse real samples. The artificial intelligence handheld sensor provides a powerful strategy for instrument-free non-skilled-personnel detection of intended analytes. However, the developed methodology still faces some difficulties. On one hand, the sensing system requires to connect external accessories to smartphone, which makes the full system become unwieldly. On the other hand, the sensing system needs a great deal of data to build capable neural network model and train the model, which will take time to establish. Nevertheless, we believe that artificial intelligence handheld sensor will successfully address current challenges in the future. It is solicitously anticipated that our research will be applied not only in environmental pollution governance and food quality management, but also in disease detection including point of care testing and smartphone-based clinics.

Liru Yan: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Bianxiang Zhang: Validation. Wei Zhou: Validation. Jiaxin Hao: Validation. Hu Shi: Software. Shuo Wang: Software. Shaomin Shuang: Validation, Funding acquisition. Lihong Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Shaomin Shuang reports that funding was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Bianxiang Zhang reports that funding was provided by Central Guidance Local Science and Technology Development Fund Project. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087) and the Central Guidance Local Science and Technology Development Fund Project (No. YDZJSX2021C001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.135083.
